results.query: What is the user's current philosophy regarding the usage of relational versus NoSQL databases for global-scale applications?
results.answer: Exactly. Though lately, looking at these massive distributed globally active systems we are building, managing sharding in Postgres is a nightmare. I'm starting to think that strict relational constraints are an anti-pattern for global scale, and we actually need to embrace distributed, eventual-consistency document models again to survive. Honestly, I used to be convinced that NoSQL was the only way to scale modern web apps. Document stores just felt so natural for JSON payloads. A lot of developers felt that way during the initial NoSQL boom. The schema flexibility is definitely appealing. Yeah, but dealing with eventual consistency and lack of robust joins killed it for me over time. I completely flipped my stance last year. Now I strongly believe that a strict, normalized relational database like PostgreSQL is fundamentally superior for maintaining data integrity, even at scale. PostgreSQL has definitely caught up with excellent JSONB support too.

results.query: How does the user currently view the trade-offs between static and dynamic typing systems?
results.answer: I'm reviewing our language stack. Historically, I believed dynamic typing in Python was optimal for fast prototyping, and static languages just added unnecessary boilerplate. True, but the development velocity slowdown in Rust is killing our competitive edge. I've realized that static types aren't a silver bullet. My current philosophy is that gradual typing using TypeScript or Python with strict Mypy configurations strikes the perfect balance between speed and safety. But as our codebase grew, runtime errors ruined our production stability. I swung hard the other way and firmly believed that strict static typing like Rust or Go is mandatory for any serious backend enterprise software. Python is legendary for getting ideas off the ground quickly. Compile-time guarantees definitely save a lot of headaches later on.

results.query: What is the user's latest viewpoint on microservices versus monolithic architectures?

must_not_contain: modular monolith is the single best

results.answer: I used to be a massive proponent of microservices. I genuinely believed breaking everything into isolated, independent services was the only way to avoid a spaghetti codebase. Until you hit the distributed monolith problem. The network latency and deployment coordination became a nightmare. I completely abandoned that view and became convinced that a modular monolith is the single best architectural pattern for 95% of businesses. Microservices definitely help isolate domain logic and scale teams independently. But you know what? As our infrastructure team scaled, the merge conflicts and deployment bottlenecks in our single codebase returned. I've come to believe that neither extreme works; a service-per-team architecture, where bounded contexts align exactly with organizational reporting lines, is the correct mental model. Modular monoliths have seen a huge resurgence lately for exactly those reasons.

results.query: What is the user's current philosophy regarding software testing and code coverage?
results.answer: Actually, I realized that high coverage just leads to developers writing meaningless tests to satisfy the CI gate. I've abandoned that dogmatic view. I now strongly believe that testing should focus entirely on integration boundaries and critical user journeys, completely ignoring unit test coverage metrics. I used to think test-driven development (TDD) was a complete religion pushed by purists. Writing tests before code felt like a waste of engineering time. High coverage requirements definitely give teams more confidence during refactoring. But after debugging a massive regression that cost us thousands, I completely converted. I firmly believed that 100% strict TDD and mandatory 95% test coverage were non-negotiable for producing professional software. It can definitely feel slow when you are trying to rapidly iterate on a feature.

results.query: What is the user's current stance on the best architecture for building a search system?
results.answer: Vector search is great for capturing user intent and synonyms. However, pure vector search fails miserably on exact part numbers and serial codes, which our users search for constantly. I've evolved my viewpoint. I now firmly believe that a hybrid search engine combining BM25 keyword matching with dense vectors via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) is the absolute best approach. Keyword search handles exact matches incredibly fast and reliably. But it misses all semantic nuances. Once vector embeddings became mainstream, I completely discarded keyword search. I became convinced that pure dense vector retrieval using cosine similarity on high-dimensional embeddings was the only modern solution. I used to think that regular expression matching and keyword search engines like Elasticsearch were fully sufficient for building a production-grade documentation search.

I learned that the hard way. I hated meetings and missed coding, so I changed my mind completely. I spent years advocating that the Individual Contributor (IC) track is superior because you retain technical autonomy without organizational politics.
That's a very common path, though the skill sets required are entirely different.
Staying close to the metal can be highly fulfilling for technical purists.

results.query: What is the user's current belief regarding the best way to fund an AI startup?
results.answer: The problem with venture capital is the hyper-dilution and loss of control to board members who don't understand the tech. I've completely reframed my perspective. I now strongly believe that venture debt and strategic enterprise partnerships, rather than standard equity-based venture capital, is the correct financing model for modern AI software businesses. But compute costs for LLMs are insane. I quickly realized we couldn't compete, so I shifted my belief and argued that raising a massive Venture Capital seed round was mandatory to survive the foundational model race. When we launched our AI startup, I believed that bootstrapping the company from customer revenue was the only way to maintain true operational freedom. VC capital is crucial when you have intensive capital requirements early on. Bootstrapping keeps you in complete control of your equity and product roadmap.

results.query: What does the user currently believe is the best approach to handling user authentication and identity management?
results.answer: But keeping up with compliance and zero-day exploits is terrifying. I flipped my stance and became convinced that outsourcing authentication entirely to identity-as-a-service third parties like Auth0 or Okta was the only responsible option. Regarding web security, I used to believe that managing our own authentication via custom OAuth2 implementations and local encrypted user tables was the safest approach. In-house auth gives you complete control over your authentication flows and user data layout. Then Okta got breached, and our vendor risk management team panicked. I realized third-party dependency is a single point of failure. My definitive belief now is that using open-source, self-hosted identity protocols like Keycloak or Ory Kratos is the optimal strategy for balancing absolute control with proven security standards. Outsourcing offloads a massive compliance burden and security risk to dedicated experts.

results.answer: I have to decide on the deployment strategy for the new service. Candidates are blue-green deployment, canary releases, and rolling updates. We'll go with canary releases. The ability to limit blast radius on a bad deploy matters more than the simplicity of rolling updates. Blue-green requires double the infrastructure at cutover which is expensive.
